journal-title-group.journal-title: Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery Global Open

title-group.article-title: Clinical Manifestations and Prognosis of Embolism Caused by Filler Injection in Different Facial Regions

contrib-group.aff: From the *Department of Ear Reconstruction, Plastic Surgery Hospital, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical College, Beijing, China
†College of Clinical Medicine, Fudan University, Xuhui District, Shanghai, China
‡Department of Cicatrix Minimally Invasive Treatment Center, Plastic Surgery Hospital, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical College, Beijing, China
§Department of Cosmetic Injection Center, Plastic Surgery Hospital, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical College, Beijing, China.

author-notes.corresp: Jintian Hu, MD, Department of Cosmetic Injection Center Plastic Surgery Hospital, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical College, Beijing, China, E-mail: hujintian@vip.163.com

permissions.copyright-statement: Copyright © 2023 The Authors. Published by Wolters Kluwer Health, Inc. on behalf of The American Society of Plastic Surgeons.

license.license-p: This is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-Non Commercial-No Derivatives License 4.0 (CCBY-NC-ND), where it is permissible to download and share the work provided it is properly cited. The work cannot be changed in any way or used commercially without permission from the journal.

sec.title: Background:
sec.p: Embolism is a serious complication after facial filling, with a usually poor prognosis of the symptoms after embolism. The authors systematically reviewed reported cases of facial vascular embolism, explored the relationship between the location and material used for facial filling and occurrence of vascular embolism, and assessed the prognosis of complications after vascular embolism.

sec.title: Methods:
sec.p: This study provides a systematic review of published cases of vascular embolism after facial filling. A summary of the filling materials and filling sites for each case, the adverse reactions and embolized blood vessels, a recording of the time when each patient experienced adverse reactions and started treatment, and a presentation of their prognosis are provided.

sec.title: Results:
sec.p: The frontal, eyebrow, and nose are common filling sites causing facial embolism. The main clinical manifestations after embolism were visual impairment, skin necrosis, and ptosis. The prognosis of visual impairment after embolization was poor, whereas skin necrosis and ptosis generally improved after treatment.

sec.title: Conclusions:
sec.p: This article aimed to review the clinical manifestations, therapies, and prognosis of embolism after facial filling. A better understanding of these complications can help clinicians to detect the occurrence of complications as early as possible and give patients timely treatment.

sec.title: Takeaways
sec.p: Question: What is the relationship between facial filling site and vascular embolism and the prognosis of complications after vascular embolism?

Findings: Frontal, eyebrow, and nose are common filling sites causing facial embolism. The main clinical manifestations after embolism were visual impairment, skin necrosis, and ptosis. The prognosis of visual impairment after embolization is poor, whereas skin necrosis and ptosis are generally improved after treatment.
Meaning: Understanding these complications can help us find out the occurrence of complications as soon as possible and provide patients timely treatment.

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Filler injection and tissue augmentation have recently become two prevailing approaches in cosmetic surgery because of their convenience, high predictability, minimal trauma, and other benefits. Commonly used materials include autologous fat and HA.1–3 Allergies, infections, granuloma formation, and vascular embolism are common complications after facial filling. Although many complications may occur after filling, overall, injection remains a relatively safe procedure if performed by well-qualified surgeons. Vascular injury is a serious complication after injection of fillers. Although previous studies have reported that the probability of vascular injury caused by soft tissue fillers is less than 0.05%,4 once vascular injury occurs, it can cause serious consequences, leading to blindness and death in the most severe cases. When fillers are accidentally injected into the vessel lumen and induce emboli, they can result in a range of serious adverse reactions such as vision loss, epidermal necrosis, ptosis, hemianopsia, and unconsciousness.5–7 This study aimed to review the clinical manifestations, treatments, and prognosis of embolism-associated adverse reactions induced by facial fillers with the view to help doctors and patients predict the prognosis of complications after embolization.

sec.title: METHODS
sec.p: This systematic review was conducted in line with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta Analyses guidelines. All articles were reviewed by three independent researchers, and eligible articles were selected. In case of doubt, three people discussed together to reach a consensus. The authors searched PubMed databases dating up to March 15, 2023, using the following Boolean string: ((artery embolization) OR (artery occlusion) OR (ischemic)) AND (injection) AND ((filler) OR (HA) OR (autologous fat)). The authors limited the cases to those reported in the English language and which summarized the injection sites. Cases unrelated to this study, without follow-up, not presented in English, with an unclear injection site or multiple injection sites, and using unknown filling materials or multiple substances were excluded from this study (Fig. 1). Data were collected on age, gender, filling site, filling materials, clinical manifestations, and patient prognosis before analysis.

caption.p: Flowchart for selecting articles for inclusion.

sec.title: RESULTS
sec.p: Among 432 studies, 68 studies reporting 165 cases of vascular embolism caused by fillers were included. Patient information is presented in Supplemental Digital Content 1. (See table, Supplemental Digital Content 1, which displays information on 165 patients.44–98 http://links.lww.com/PRSGO/C749).

Data on the filling materials, clinical diagnoses, and symptoms at different filling sites are presented in Tables 1–3, respectively. The injection sites (Fig. 2) were the forehead (n = 37), glabellum (n = 32), nose (n = 61), periorbital area (n = 2), cheek (n = 3), temporal area (n = 13), chin (n = 2), lip (n = 1), eyelid (n = 2), and nasolabial fold (n = 10). The main diagnoses for facial vascular embolisms were central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO), posterior ciliary artery occlusion (PCAO), ophthalmic artery occlusion (OAO), and acute cerebral infarction. (See figure, Supplemental Digital Content 2, which displays proportions of different diagnoses. http://links.lww.com/PRSGO/C748.) Vascular embolisms of largest proportion were caused by injections made in the nasal region (37.0%). The material most commonly found to cause embolism was hyaluronic acid (HA). Among the 165 patients, visual impairment was the most common symptom (n = 142, 86.1%), whereas epidermal damage tended to occur after vascular occlusion (42.4%). Of the 123 HA injections, 59 (48.0%) resulted in skin necrosis. All eight cases in which autologous fat was used reported visual damage; however, these cases did not show significant skin necrosis. In addition, some patients also experienced symptoms such as hair loss, paralysis, and disturbance of consciousness. After treatment, eight of 36 patients who experienced visual impairment due to forehead filling improved their vision, and eight of 32 patients with visual impairment caused by glabella filling improved their vision. In addition, 14 of 58 patients who experienced visual impairment due to nasal filling improved their vision after treatment. Two of four visually-impaired patients who received facial fillers on their temples showed improved vision. One patient developed residual HA in the anterior chamber and visual impairment after facial filling, and their vision ultimately improved. Six of the 10 patients injected in the nasolabial fold showed visual impairment, and none of them showed later visual improvement. Two patients injected in the periorbital area showed no visual improvement. Data on cases with improved vision are presented in Table 4. In addition, filling in the chin could block the lingual artery, resulting in atrophy of the tongue.

caption.p: Types of Fillers in Different Areas

fn.p: AF, autologous fat; CaHA, calcium hydroxylapatite; PMMA, polymethylmethacrylate microspheres; PLLA, poly L-lactic acid.

caption.p: Diagnosis of Fillers in Different Areas
Symptoms of Fillers in Different Areas
Information about Patients with Improved Vision

tr.td: Residual hyaluronic acid in the anterior chamber

fn.p: AION, anterior ischemic optic neuropathy; CaHA; calcium hydroxylapatite; CF, counting finger; ChAO, choroidal artery occlusion; F, feminine; HM, hand motion; LP, light perception; NLP, no light perception; OIS, ocular ischemic syndrome; PMMA, polymethylmethacrylate microspheres; SOVO, superior ophthalmic vein occlusion; STAO, superficial temporal artery occlusion.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: Facial filler injections into the vasculature can induce serious complications.8–10 The main reasons why facial fillers cause vascular embolism or ischemia after facial filling are partial or complete occlusion caused by the injection of the filler into the blood vessel in the direction of the blood flow or against the blood flow, or the accumulation of the filler on the outer side of the blood vessel, which compresses the blood vessel. Vision loss was the most common adverse reaction in all the vascular occlusion cases reported, mainly caused by OAO, BRAO, PCAO, and CRAO.2,3,7,9,11 The majority of cases in which facial filling eventually led to blindness did not show adequate improvement after treatment because of retina intolerance to ischemia.1,12,13 Of the 142 cases of visual damage, only 33 patients showed improvement, and the final visual acuity remained inferior. Besides visual impairment, skin necrosis and ptosis are common symptoms of vessel occlusion.14–16 However, emboli-induced skin necrosis and ptosis usually show a better prognosis after appropriate treatment.17,18 Only a few cases of severe necrosis can be treated using full-thickness skin grafts.15 In addition to ordinary fillers, we found that lidocaine and adrenaline solutions can induce intravascular embolisms and ultimately elicit blindness. A previous study suggested that this may be the result of platelet aggregation and thrombosis after vasospasm.19 Among the cases with HA augmentations, 59 (48.0 %) cases of skin necrosis were reported. Among 40 patients with facial fat filling, only four (10.0%) had skin necrosis. This may be because fat granules are larger, and thus tend to block the proximal branch of the vasculature. HA granules are smaller, and thus tend to block the distal end and absorb water, ultimately causing swelling and compressing small vessels.20 This observation does not imply that skin necrosis will not occur with lipid fillers. However, we suggest that lipid-induced necrosis may be less severe than HA-induced necrosis. In addition, when unilateral continuous skin necrosis occurs after facial filling, the doctors need to consider the possibility of vascular embolism, and when the skin lesion is unilateral erythema, they need to consider that it may be caused by herpes zoster.15,21 Unlike blindness, hair loss does not occur immediately. Hair loss is a delayed condition. Patients with hair loss after temporal filling usually experience hair loss 1–2 weeks after filling, and gradually develop a complete hair loss area after 1 month. After 2–3 months, the hair that falls off gradually grows back, with hair density returning to normal levels by 5–6 months.18

Subcutaneous injection of hyaluronidase is a common treatment method for blindness caused by facial filling. In addition, retrobulbar injection of hyaluronidase is the standard treatment for hyaluronidase to block the ophthalmic artery, which has proved to be somewhat effective in practice.22,23 However, in vitro experiments have shown that hyaluronidase cannot penetrate the optic nerve sheath, nor the arterial wall,15,24 and is thus unable to dissolve emboli in the lumen of the central retinal artery. Other experiments have also failed to demonstrate that hyaluronidase can dissolve HA blocking in the optic nerve within 24 hours.25 On the other hand, the dissolution of HA induced by hyaluronidase is a relatively slow process usually exceeding 90 minutes, after which the optic nerve is irreversibly impaired by ischemia, even if HA is ultimately dissolved.26 Other than the lack of convincing evidence, under the consideration that unnecessary injections may worsen patients’ condition by inducing hemorrhage, Chiang et al27 have recommended that this method be applied with extreme caution by well-qualified clinicians. Nonetheless, without other effective treatments available, high-dose retrobulbar hyaluronidase injection is a mainstream therapy for HA-associated OAO.23 If the exact branches or sites of the vessel occluded by HA can be identified, it is recommended to inject hyaluronidase into the upstream arteries for intravascular dissolution.12,17 A new study has suggested that intravascular HA can contribute to thrombosis, thus generating combined HA-thrombus embolization. Hence, injection of hyaluronidase combined with urokinase could be a beneficial approach, as urokinase is a traditional plasminogen activator and thrombolytic agent with a specific treatment effect on secondary thrombosis and coagulation.28–30 Simultaneously, considering that vascular occlusion may be elicited by vasospasm and inflammation, a combination of hyaluronidase and glucocorticoid can also be a sound treatment option.31

The treatment of autologous fat-associated embolism is hampered by the lack of effective thrombolytics,32 whereas the thrombolytic treatment of HA-associated embolism lacks supporting evidence.33 Under such circumstances, Tansatit et al34 reported improving the technique for arterial bypass surgery, and Wang et al35 successfully used intraarterial mechanical recanalization, with both of these approaches showing significant potential. Recently, hyperbaric oxygen therapy has been found to significantly improve the oxygen supply at the embolic site, thereby inhibiting tissue necrosis.36 However, the effect of hyperbaric oxygen therapy on visual improvement remains controversial.37,38

In addition to thrombolytic therapy, patients should take oral aspirin and anti-inflammatory drugs and analgesics if necessary; after injection, the diffusion of hyaluronidase can be enhanced through massage.23,37

HA structures with different degrees of cross-linking produce different effects. Thus, HA with a high cross-linking degree contains more cross-linking bonds, resulting in a higher molecular weight and viscosity. This denser HA can be used to fill deep wrinkles or sunken areas, such as the nasolabial sulcus and lacrimal sulcus, effectively increasing tissue volume and forming a stronger supporting structure. HA with a low cross-linking degree contains fewer cross-linking bonds, and its molecular weight and viscosity are smaller. This softer HA can be used to fill fine wrinkles or surface layers of the skin, such as the lips and neck, effectively increasing skin moisture and improving skin elasticity and glossiness. Crosslinked HA can show greater stability and durability, and different cross-linking degrees can yield different properties, making them suitable for filling different parts. However, existing research has not found significant differences in the safety of HA with different cross-linking degrees.39 Unfortunately, data on the cross-linking degree of HA have rarely been reported in the cases included, so this study could not compare the safety of injection under different cross-linking degrees.

Fast injection can cause HA to exceed blood pressure and retrograde into vessels if blood vessels are impaired during injection; thus, the injection should be performed slowly and softly, with the volume of the filler being less than 0.1 mL each time.40 Using small needles has been suggested to reduce the pressure and speed of filler injection, thereby reducing the opportunity of reflux into the blood vessels.41 On the other hand, using bigger cannulas has been suggested as the safer option, as blunt cannulas with larger diameters can avoid puncturing blood vessels.42 Extracting blood to observe if its potential reflux may be effective to determine whether the blood vessels are impaired or not; however, the damage could be so minor that the technicians may not be able to observe the slight hemorrhage, while HA may still be able to penetrate into the blood vessels due to its hydrophilicity, and eventually to aggregate into large emboli.43 With fillers injected in the neck and face having the potential to elicit serious, sometimes fatal complications, the technician must have a comprehensive understanding of the vascular distribution and variation, discuss potential complications with the patient in advance, and pay close attention to the patient’s reaction during the operation.40

Compared with previous studies, this study included more cases, explored the differences in facial embolism caused by different filler compositions and different filling sites, recorded the time of adverse reactions and the start of treatment, and described the prognosis of these cases. Nonetheless, this study has several limitations. First, there were significant differences in the follow-up time and treatment methods among the studies, which could affect the reported prognosis results. Second, due to the lack of detailed descriptions about the time that elapsed between the filling and symptom appearance, as well as the time between the symptom appearance and treatment, this study did not discuss the impact of these factors on prognosis.

sec.title: CONCLUSIONS
sec.p: Facial fillers such as HA, autologous fat, and collagen can cause facial vascular embolism. Embolization of blood vessels can lead to clinical manifestations such as impaired vision, skin necrosis, and ptosis. Early detection of embolisms can help better treat patients.

sec.title: DISCLOSURE
sec.p: The authors have no financial interests to declare in relation to the content of this article.

p.italic: Disclosure statements are at the end of this article, following the correspondence information.

fn.p: Related Digital Media are available in the full-text version of the article on www.PRSGlobalOpen.com.
Drs. Zhuang and Qiaoyuan contributed equally to this work.
